translationBlock.key: Find a bug in this page? %sEdit this page yourself, then submit a pull request.
Installation
Know the assumptions you are making, and test assumptions that matter

These types of errors are usually more straightforward to fix, since the compiler can usually point us right at the code in question. Regardless, the code isn't actually running at this point, so our runtime debugging techniques aren't applicable here.
"Debugging is twice as hard as writing the code in the first place. Therefore, if you write the code as cleverly as possible, you are, by definition, not smart enough to debug it." --Brian Kernighan
We don't know offhand what @value is, so it may be useful to output the value before we use it, especially if it's involved in part of the stack trace chain.
Bugs in MethodScript itself
You can also use %s for a more detailed output, which includes the variable name. So, assuming @value contained 'string', then %strace(@value)%s would output: %s@value: string%s.

If when we trace(@value1), we see that it is "test1", then we can safely assume that @value2 is 0. However, if we did not have that trace() present, (allowing us to know the value of @value1 for sure) we must be aware that we have two assumptions here. If the assumptions matter, that is, they are involved in the code that is having problems, we would be wise to test those assumptions, in order to rule out the problem.

MethodScript is itself written in code, and is prone to bugs as well. These debugging techniques are not meant to be useful when you get a java stack trace, and in fact, the java stack trace often doesn't help you identify the problem at all. In these cases, you may be able to find a workaround which prevents the error, but in ALL cases if you get a Java stack trace, this is a bug which should be reported. An example Java stack trace looks like this:

These debugging techniques do not help with syntax errors. There is a difference between compiler errors and runtime errors. Syntax errors do not require testing values or anything, because they are a problem with your syntax, not your logic. For instance, in the following code, we are missing a parenthesis.
As you look at the problem code, it is likely that you won't test all your assumptions. Sometimes, it's obvious what a particular value is, for instance, given this code:

The most important thing for you to know is how the code is supposed to work. Granted, there may be a problem with how it is actually working, but if you don't know what the code is supposed to do, you can't know what is wrong with it. This includes usages of the builtin API, as well as your own code. Stack traces are important to understand as well. Lets examine the components of a stack trace, given the following source code:

If @b is zero, then this would certainly cause the error. However, there is more to the stack trace, which is useful, because this line may not be where we actually want to fix the error. The next element in the stack trace tells us that _proc2 was called from _proc1, on line 4.

This is our ''stack trace''. The problem is that we are dividing by zero. The first line of the stack trace tells us what the error type was, and gives us the exception message as well. As you can see, the root of our problem is on line 8, if @b is 0. @b ends up being 0, so we end up dividing by zero, thus the exception. The root cause is always the first element in the stack trace. Each stack trace element follows the format <code description>:<file path>:<line number>. In this case, the example was run from the command line, so the "main" file is run from the Interpreter, but normally this will contain a file path. The important information is the code description and the line number. The code description tells us that this is code defined in the procedure "_proc2", and the error is on line 8.
When we run this script, we get the following error:
Understand code flow

Ok, so this still doesn't help us much, because we don't know where @a came from, so lets dig down again into the stack trace. The last element is <<main code>>, which means that this is the "top level" of the execution unit. In this case, it means that the code is "loose" code, that is, it is not contained inside a procedure.

Debugging your code is an important, but challenging part of writing working code. Currently, MethodScript does not have an interactive debugger (though this is planned in the future) however, this does not stop you from debugging scripts anyways. When debugging, there are several key concepts
If you can determine what was done to cause this error, please include that information in the bug report, but often times, this error can be fixed without any information other than the stack trace.

Aha! Now we see where the 0 is coming from, it is hard coded in at this point. Sometimes your code is more complicated though, and it's not so obvious where a value is coming from, which leads us to our next section:

Sometimes it's not obvious where a variable's values come from. In this case, you can use runtime checks to gather the value of variables. The simplest way to do this is to use %s or %s to output the value of the variable at that moment in time. Say you have a value that is brought in from storage:
Syntax errors
Understand variable's values
About
Home
Privacy Policy
Sponsors
Help
Team. All rights reserved.
Docs
While paused, the Variables panel shows all variables in the current scope. You can expand arrays to inspect their contents.
Threading mode: %ssync%s (default) blocks the executing thread at breakpoints, %sasync%s snapshots state without blocking.
Minecraft Server Debugging
debug-server-bind-address
Breakpoints
'''Continue''' (F5) - Resume execution until the next breakpoint.
When stepping into a line that calls multiple procedures, VS Code will show step-into targets so you can choose which procedure to enter.
debug-server-threading
debug-server-timeout
The address the debug server binds to. Use %s0.0.0.0%s for remote connections (requires KEYPAIR security).
Regardless of whether you use the VS Code debugger, there are some key concepts to keep in mind when debugging.
Command Line Debugging
The recommended way to debug MethodScript is with the official VS Code extension. It provides a full-featured debugging experience using the [[%s|Debug Adapter Protocol (DAP)]], including:
debug-server-security
--debug
Shorthand for %s--debug-security NONE --debug-bind-address 0.0.0.0%s. For quick local testing only.
off%s disables the debug server. %son%s starts it automatically when the plugin loads. %smanual%s allows starting it with %s/debug-server start%s.
Variable inspection (locals and closure variables)
Keypair Security
debug-server-mode
async%s snapshots state without blocking the server thread (default, recommended). %ssync%s blocks the executing thread at breakpoints.
When attaching from VS Code, select "Keypair" security and provide your private key (or leave blank to auto-detect from %s~/.ssh/%s).
Stepping
KEYPAIR%s requires SSH-style authentication and enables TLS (recommended). %sNONE%s allows unauthenticated connections, restricted to localhost.
VS Code Debugger
--debug-insecure
Right-click a breakpoint to add a condition (the breakpoint only fires when the condition is true) or convert it to a log point (outputs a message without pausing).
Install the [[%s|MethodScript VS Code extension]] from the VS Code Marketplace by searching for "MethodScript" in the Extensions panel, or install it from the command line:
'''Step Into''' (F11) - If the current line calls a procedure, step into it.
Address to bind to. Defaults to 127.0.0.1.
debug-server-port
off

Once the debug server is running, press F5 in VS Code, choose "Attach", and enter the host and port. If using keypair security, you will also need to provide your private key. By default, this looks for ´~/.ssh/id_rsa`.
For remote debugging keypair security is required, but can also be used locally. It uses SSH-style key authentication and TLS encryption.
The server will automatically shut down after the configured timeout (default 15 minutes) if no client connects.

Debugging your code is an important, but challenging part of writing working code. MethodScript has an interactive debugger, offered through the official VS Code extension, which supports breakpoints, step execution, variable inspection, and more. This page covers how to set up and use the debugger, as well as general debugging techniques.
--debug-bind-address <address>
Once paused at a breakpoint, use the debug toolbar to control execution:
Call stack inspection
Step over, step into, step out
--debug-port <port>
--debug-suspend
'''Step Out''' (Shift+F11) - Run until the current procedure returns.
You can also launch the debugger manually from the command line:
KEYPAIR
To set it up:
Minutes before the debug server shuts down with no connected client. Set to 0 to disable. Only applies to manual mode.
To debug a standalone %s.ms%s script, open it in VS Code and press F5, then "Launch". The extension will launch the script with the debug server enabled and automatically connect.
Generate an SSH key pair if you don't have one: %sssh-keygen -t ed25519%s (or %smscript -- key-gen%s).
Using the Debugger
Variable Inspection
'''Step Over''' (F10) - Execute the current line and pause at the next line.
From command line, use %s--debug-security KEYPAIR%s. For CommandHelper, set %sdebug-server-security%s to %sKEYPAIR%s in preferences.ini.
Click in the gutter next to a line number in VS Code to set a breakpoint. When execution reaches that line, the debugger will pause and let you inspect the current state.
Breakpoints (line and conditional)
The TCP port for the debug server.
Log points (output a message without stopping)
Security mode: %sNONE%s (localhost only, no auth) or %sKEYPAIR%s (SSH-style auth + TLS).
This starts the debug server on port 6732 (the default). You can then attach VS Code to it by pressing F5 and choosing "Attach". The following command line flags are available:
--debug-security <mode>
Wait for a debugger to connect before executing the script.
Manual mode (%sdebug-server-mode=manual%s) lets you start and stop the debug server on demand from the server console:
To debug startup scripts (e.g. %smain.ms%s), connect the debugger first, then use %s/recompile%s to re-run the startup scripts with the debugger attached.

Sometimes it's not obvious where a variable's values come from. If you are using the VS Code debugger, you can simply set a breakpoint and inspect variables in the Variables panel. Without a debugger, you can use runtime checks to gather the value of variables. The simplest way to do this is to use %s or %s to output the value of the variable at that moment in time. Say you have a value that is brought in from storage:

Copy your public key to the authorized debug keys file at %s~/.mscript/authorized_debug_keys%s (for command line) or %splugins/CommandHelper/authorized_debug_keys%s (for CommandHelper). One public key per line, same format as %s~/.ssh/authorized_keys%s.
Enable the DAP debug server.
--debug-threading-mode <mode>
General Debugging Techniques
Manual Mode
The port for the debug server. Defaults to 6732.
async
To debug scripts running inside a Minecraft server, configure the debug server in %splugins/CommandHelper/preferences.ini%s:
